callout.text: Docker is most valuable when tied to measurable reliability or velocity outcomes — not checkbox adoption.

theory: Docker addresses Images, containers, Dockerfile, volumes, networking. in modern DevOps systems.
Engineering teams adopt Docker when manual processes or legacy tooling block reliability, auditability, or scale.
Core mental model: understand components, failure domains, data flow, and the operational interfaces (CLI, API, UI) you will use daily.
Production rollouts require idempotent automation, peer review, staged apply, and documented rollback — treat changes as production code.
Interviewers want STAR stories linking Docker to measurable outcomes: fewer outages, faster deploys, lower cost, or reduced toil.

interview.q: When would you introduce Docker on a greenfield project?
interview.a: When the team feels recurring pain around the problem Docker solves — not before you have basic CI and observability.

interview.q: What metrics prove Docker is working?
interview.a: Lower MTTR, higher deploy frequency, fewer manual runbook steps, or improved SLO adherence — pick one primary metric upfront.

interview.q: Biggest production failure mode with Docker?
interview.a: Untested config promoted fleet-wide without canary/serial strategy; missing rollback and unclear ownership during incidents.

interview.q: How do you test Docker changes safely?
interview.a: Staging mirrors prod constraints, dry-run/plan modes, peer review, and incremental rollout with automated health checks.

interview.q: How does Docker fit on-call?
interview.a: Document alerts, escalation, and rollback; ensure dashboards show golden signals (latency, errors, saturation) for affected services.

interview.q: Beginner mistake with Docker?
interview.a: Copying a tutorial config without adapting to your network, secrets, and compliance boundaries.

interview.q: Intermediate: multi-environment strategy?
interview.a: Separate state/credentials per env, promote artifacts not ad-hoc edits, and keep prod changes behind approval gates.

interview.q: Advanced: blast radius control?
interview.a: Batch size limits, feature flags, automatic rollback on SLO burn, and isolated failure domains.

bestPractices: Version-control all Docker configuration and review changes like application code.
Automate validation (lint, policy checks) in CI before production apply.
Document ownership, on-call runbooks, and rollback for every production change.
Use least privilege for credentials and rotate secrets regularly.
Measure before/after: error rate, deploy time, cost, or toil hours saved.
mistakes: Adopting Docker without a written problem statement or success metric.
Skipping staging that mirrors production networking and data volume.
No rollback path — forward-only changes during incidents.
Alert noise without actionable runbooks — on-call ignores pages.

interview.q: When would you introduce Redis on a greenfield project?
interview.a: When the team feels recurring pain around the problem Redis solves — not before you have basic CI and observability.

interview.q: What metrics prove Redis is working?
interview.a: Lower MTTR, higher deploy frequency, fewer manual runbook steps, or improved SLO adherence — pick one primary metric upfront.

interview.q: Biggest production failure mode with Redis?
interview.a: Untested config promoted fleet-wide without canary/serial strategy; missing rollback and unclear ownership during incidents.

interview.q: How do you test Redis changes safely?
interview.a: Staging mirrors prod constraints, dry-run/plan modes, peer review, and incremental rollout with automated health checks.

interview.q: How does Redis fit on-call?
interview.a: Document alerts, escalation, and rollback; ensure dashboards show golden signals (latency, errors, saturation) for affected services.

interview.q: Beginner mistake with Redis?
interview.a: Copying a tutorial config without adapting to your network, secrets, and compliance boundaries.

interview.q: Intermediate: multi-environment strategy?
interview.a: Separate state/credentials per env, promote artifacts not ad-hoc edits, and keep prod changes behind approval gates.

interview.q: Advanced: blast radius control?
interview.a: Batch size limits, feature flags, automatic rollback on SLO burn, and isolated failure domains.

bestPractices: Version-control all Redis configuration and review changes like application code.
Automate validation (lint, policy checks) in CI before production apply.
Document ownership, on-call runbooks, and rollback for every production change.
Use least privilege for credentials and rotate secrets regularly.
Measure before/after: error rate, deploy time, cost, or toil hours saved.
mistakes: Adopting Redis without a written problem statement or success metric.
Skipping staging that mirrors production networking and data volume.
No rollback path — forward-only changes during incidents.
Alert noise without actionable runbooks — on-call ignores pages.

interview.q: When would you introduce Linux Basics on a greenfield project?

interview.q: Biggest production failure mode with Linux Basics?
interview.a: Untested config promoted fleet-wide without canary/serial strategy; missing rollback and unclear ownership during incidents.

interview.q: How do you test Linux Basics changes safely?
interview.a: Staging mirrors prod constraints, dry-run/plan modes, peer review, and incremental rollout with automated health checks.

interview.q: How does Linux Basics fit on-call?
interview.a: Document alerts, escalation, and rollback; ensure dashboards show golden signals (latency, errors, saturation) for affected services.

interview.q: Beginner mistake with Linux Basics?
interview.a: Copying a tutorial config without adapting to your network, secrets, and compliance boundaries.

interview.q: Intermediate: multi-environment strategy?
interview.a: Separate state/credentials per env, promote artifacts not ad-hoc edits, and keep prod changes behind approval gates.

interview.q: Advanced: blast radius control?
interview.a: Batch size limits, feature flags, automatic rollback on SLO burn, and isolated failure domains.

callout.text: AWS is API-driven infrastructure — understand IAM, regions/AZs, and billing tags before clicking "launch".

theory: Amazon Web Services spans 30+ regions; each region has multiple Availability Zones (isolated data centers). Place workloads across AZs for HA; use regions for latency and compliance.
EC2 provides virtual machines (instance types: compute, memory, GPU optimized). Auto Scaling Groups + ALB replace failed instances. Use launch templates, not one-off AMIs.
S3 is object storage (11 nines durability). Buckets are global names; enable versioning, encryption (SSE-S3/KMS), and block public access by default.
RDS manages relational databases (Postgres, MySQL). Multi-AZ sync replica for failover; read replicas for scale. Backups and maintenance windows are automated.
Lambda runs event-driven functions — pay per invocation and GB-second. Cold starts matter for latency; use provisioned concurrency for steady traffic.
IAM is the permission system: users, roles, policies (JSON). Prefer roles for EC2/Lambda over long-lived access keys. Least privilege with policy boundaries.
VPC isolates networks: subnets (public/private), route tables, NAT gateway for outbound private traffic, security groups (stateful) vs NACLs (stateless).
CloudWatch collects metrics, logs, and alarms. Wire alarms to SNS/PagerDuty. Cost Explorer and budgets prevent surprise bills.
devops/aws-basics.diagram:
Users --> Route53 / CloudFront
              |
              v
           ALB (public subnet)
              |
         EC2 ASG (private)
         /          \
        v            v
     RDS Multi-AZ   ElastiCache
        |
     S3 (assets, backups)
        |
   CloudWatch / IAM roles

examples.code:
aws s3 mb s3://my-app-assets-prod
aws s3 cp ./dist s3://my-app-assets-prod/ --recursive

aws ec2 describe-instances \
  --filters "Name=tag:Environment,Values=prod" \
  --query "Reservations[].Instances[].InstanceId"

interview.q: Region vs Availability Zone?
interview.a: Region is geographic; AZ is isolated DC within region. Spread ASG across 2+ AZs for HA.

interview.q: Security Group vs NACL?
interview.a: SG is stateful, instance-level, allow rules. NACL is stateless, subnet-level, allow+deny.

interview.q: When Lambda vs EC2?
interview.a: Lambda for spiky/short tasks and ops glue; EC2 for long-running, custom kernel, or steady high CPU.

interview.q: S3 consistency model?
interview.a: Read-after-write for new objects; strong read consistency for overwrites/deletes (since 2020).

interview.q: IAM role vs access key?
interview.a: Roles rotate automatically via STS — preferred for apps on AWS; keys are static secrets.

interview.q: RDS failover?
interview.a: Multi-AZ: automatic DNS failover to standby; brief disconnect — apps must retry.

interview.q: How to reduce AWS bill?
interview.a: Right-size instances, Savings Plans/RI, S3 lifecycle to Glacier, delete idle EIPs/NAT, tagging for chargeback.

interview.q: What is VPC peering?
interview.a: Private routing between two VPCs — non-transitive; often use Transit Gateway at scale.

interview.q: FAANG: design multi-region active-active?
interview.a: Route53 latency routing, DynamoDB global tables or conflict-aware DB, S3 CRR, careful session stickiness.

interview.q: Shared responsibility model?
interview.a: AWS secures cloud; you secure data, IAM, OS patches on EC2, app code, security group rules.

bestPractices: Enable MFA on root; use IAM Identity Center
Tag every resource (env, team, cost-center)
Private subnets for app/DB tiers
Encrypt data at rest and in transit
Use Infrastructure as Code (Terraform/CDK)
Set billing alarms
mistakes: Public S3 buckets
Root access keys in CI
Single-AZ RDS for production
Oversized instances without metrics review
NAT Gateway in every dev VPC 24/7

callout.text: Azure organizes everything under subscriptions and resource groups — IAM via Entra ID (formerly Azure AD) is the security backbone.

theory: Azure Resource Manager (ARM) is the deployment plane — resources live in resource groups within a subscription. Tags drive cost allocation and policy.
Virtual Machines pair with Virtual Networks (VNets), subnets, NSGs (firewall rules), and Azure Load Balancer or Application Gateway for traffic.
Azure Blob Storage provides hot/cool/archive tiers for objects; Azure Files offers SMB shares. Use managed identities instead of storage keys in apps.
Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) hosts container workloads — control plane is managed; you pay for node pools. Integrate with ACR for images and Key Vault for secrets.
Azure Functions is the serverless compute tier (consumption or premium plans). Bind to Event Grid, Service Bus, or HTTP triggers; watch cold start on consumption.
Entra ID provides SSO, conditional access, and RBAC across subscriptions. Prefer managed identities for Azure-to-Azure auth.
Monitor with Azure Monitor + Log Analytics; Application Insights for APM. Set action groups for alert routing.

callout.text: Ansible is agentless SSH automation — idempotent playbooks describe desired state, not imperative steps.

theory: Ansible connects to managed nodes over SSH (or WinRM) and pushes modules that declare desired state. No agent daemon runs on targets — only Python and an SSH user with sudo.
Playbooks are YAML ordered lists of plays; each play targets an inventory group and applies roles or tasks. Roles bundle tasks, handlers, templates, and defaults for reuse.
Inventory defines hosts and groups (static INI/YAML or dynamic from cloud APIs). Variables layer: extra-vars > play vars > role defaults > facts.
Idempotency means re-running a playbook should make zero changes when the system already matches (changed=0). Modules like apt, copy, and systemd check state before acting.
Ansible Vault encrypts secrets inside repo files. ansible-vault encrypt/decrypt integrates with CI using vault password files or env vars — never plaintext prod secrets in git.
Handlers run once at end of play when notified (e.g. restart nginx after config template changes). Tags limit execution: ansible-playbook site.yml --tags web.
Tower/AWX (now Ansible Automation Platform) adds RBAC, job templates, scheduling, and audit trails for enterprise teams.
Compared to Terraform (provisioning) and Chef/Puppet (agent-based), Ansible wins for ad-hoc fleet patching and app config when SSH access exists.
devops/ansible.diagram:
Control node (ansible-playbook)
         | SSH / WinRM
         v
  +------------------+
  |  Inventory       |
  |  web, db, cache  |
  +------------------+
         |
    +----+----+----+
    v    v    v    v
  web1 web2 db1 cache1
         |
    Modules: apt, copy,
    template, systemd

examples.code:
[web]
web1 ansible_host=10.0.1.10
web2 ansible_host=10.0.1.11

[web:vars]
ansible_user=deploy

- hosts: web
  become: true
  tasks:
    - name: Install nginx
      apt:
        name: nginx
        state: present
    - name: Deploy site config
      template:
        src: nginx.conf.j2
        dest: /etc/nginx/nginx.conf
      notify: Reload nginx
  handlers:
    - name: Reload nginx
      systemd:
        name: nginx
        state: reloaded

ansible web -m ping
ansible-playbook site.yml --check --diff
ansible-vault encrypt group_vars/prod/secrets.yml

interview.q: Why is Ansible called agentless?
interview.a: It uses SSH/WinRM to push modules; no persistent agent on nodes reduces operational overhead and firewall complexity.

interview.q: Explain idempotency with an example.
interview.a: apt: name=nginx state=present on an already-installed host reports ok/changed=0; only missing packages trigger change.

interview.q: Handlers vs tasks?
interview.a: Tasks run every time (unless skipped); handlers run once at play end when notified — ideal for service restarts.

interview.q: How do you manage secrets?
interview.a: Ansible Vault encrypts YAML blobs; decrypt at runtime with vault password from CI secret store.

interview.q: Dynamic inventory use case?
interview.a: Query AWS/GCP APIs so new autoscaled instances join groups without manual INI edits.

interview.q: Ansible vs Terraform?
interview.a: Terraform provisions infrastructure (API CRUD); Ansible configures OS/apps on existing hosts — often used together.

interview.q: What is a role?
interview.a: Reusable unit: tasks/, handlers/, templates/, defaults/ — shared across playbooks like a package.

interview.q: Debugging failed tasks?
interview.a: Run with -vvv, check register variables, use ansible-playbook --start-at-task, verify become and connectivity.

interview.q: FAANG: scale limits?
interview.a: Forking SSH to thousands of hosts needs strategy: rolling batches, mitogen/mirror acceleration, tower for parallelism control.

interview.q: Rolling deploy pattern?
interview.a: Serial or max_fail_percentage in play; update subset, health check, continue — avoids full-fleet bad config.

bestPractices: Use roles and group_vars/host_vars hierarchy
Always --check on prod-like staging first
Pin collection versions in requirements.yml
Limit become: true to tasks that need it
Tag tasks for partial runs
Store vault files separately with tight ACL
mistakes: Running playbooks without become when root needed
command/shell instead of idempotent modules
Secrets in plain group_vars committed to git
No serial strategy — all hosts break at once
Ignoring ansible-lint warnings

callout.text: An API gateway is the front door for clients — centralize cross-cutting concerns so microservices stay focused on domain logic.

theory: API gateways terminate TLS, authenticate clients (JWT, API keys, mTLS), route to backend services, and enforce rate limits and quotas.
They enable protocol translation (REST to gRPC), request/response transformation, and API versioning without changing every service.
BFF (Backend for Frontend) pattern: separate gateway graphs for mobile vs web clients to reduce over-fetching and tailor payloads.
Kong, AWS API Gateway, Envoy/Gateway API, and NGINX are common implementations — pick based on latency, ops model, and plugin ecosystem.
Gateways cache responses where safe (GET with Cache-Control), collapsing thundering herds on hot endpoints.
Observability: per-route metrics, distributed tracing headers (traceparent), and structured access logs feed SLO dashboards.
Failure modes: gateway becomes single point of failure — run multi-AZ, health-check upstreams, circuit-break when backends degrade.

  service service service

    plugins:

        config: { minute: 100 }

interview.q: Gateway vs load balancer?
interview.a: LB distributes traffic L4/L7; gateway adds auth, routing, transformation, developer portal, and API product features.

interview.q: How prevent gateway overload?
interview.a: Rate limits, circuit breakers, autoscale gateway pool, cache idempotent GETs, shed load with 429 + Retry-After.

interview.q: Design Stripe-like public API.
interview.a: Versioned routes, API keys + OAuth, idempotency keys, per-key rate limits, webhooks, comprehensive audit logs.

tradeoffs: Central gateway simplifies policy but can become a monolith — mitigate with strict plugin governance and horizontal scale.
Synchronous aggregation at gateway increases latency vs client-side parallel calls — aggregate only when round-trip savings justify it.
Managed cloud gateways reduce ops but add vendor lock-in and per-request cost at extreme scale.
mTLS everywhere is secure but painful for mobile/public clients — often terminate TLS at gateway and use JWT inside.

callout.text: Cassandra optimizes for write-heavy, multi-datacenter availability — design around partition keys first, queries second.

theory: Apache Cassandra is a distributed wide-column store (partition key + clustering columns). Data is partitioned with consistent hashing across nodes.
Tunable consistency per query: ONE, QUORUM, ALL — trade latency vs correctness. Lightweight transactions (LWT) use Paxos for compare-and-set.
Replication Factor (RF) copies data across nodes; NetworkTopologyStrategy places replicas per datacenter for local reads.
Writes go to commit log then memtable; SSTables flush to disk with periodic compaction (size-tiered, leveled).
CQL resembles SQL but multi-table JOINs are absent — model denormalized tables per query pattern (one table per query).
Netflix and Apple use Cassandra for time-series-ish, high-ingest workloads where Postgres would struggle to scale writes horizontally.
Operations: nodetool repair, rebuild, decommission; watch compaction lag, pending hints, and p99 read latency.
db/cassandra.diagram:
Client (CQL)
      |
  Coordinator node
      |
  +---+---+---+
  v   v   v   v
  N1  N2  N3  N4   (ring)
  RF=3 replicas per DC

examples.code: INSERT INTO events (bucket, ts, data) VALUES (?, ?, ?) USING QUORUM;

interview.q: What is a partition key?
interview.a: Determines which node owns the row; all rows with same partition key live together — hot partitions limit scale.

interview.q: QUORUM meaning?
interview.a: Majority of replicas (typically (RF/2)+1) must respond — balances consistency and availability.

interview.q: When NOT to use Cassandra?
interview.a: Heavy ad-hoc analytics, multi-table transactions, or workloads needing rich relational joins.

callout.text: Graph Databases is most valuable when tied to measurable reliability or velocity outcomes — not checkbox adoption.

theory: Graph Databases addresses When graphs win, model patterns, use cases. in modern Database Systems systems.
Engineering teams adopt Graph Databases when manual processes or legacy tooling block reliability, auditability, or scale.
Core mental model: understand components, failure domains, data flow, and the operational interfaces (CLI, API, UI) you will use daily.
Production rollouts require idempotent automation, peer review, staged apply, and documented rollback — treat changes as production code.
Interviewers want STAR stories linking Graph Databases to measurable outcomes: fewer outages, faster deploys, lower cost, or reduced toil.

interview.q: When would you introduce Graph Databases on a greenfield project?
interview.a: When the team feels recurring pain around the problem Graph Databases solves — not before you have basic CI and observability.

interview.q: What metrics prove Graph Databases is working?
interview.a: Lower MTTR, higher deploy frequency, fewer manual runbook steps, or improved SLO adherence — pick one primary metric upfront.

interview.q: Biggest production failure mode with Graph Databases?
interview.a: Untested config promoted fleet-wide without canary/serial strategy; missing rollback and unclear ownership during incidents.

interview.q: How do you test Graph Databases changes safely?
interview.a: Staging mirrors prod constraints, dry-run/plan modes, peer review, and incremental rollout with automated health checks.

interview.q: How does Graph Databases fit on-call?
interview.a: Document alerts, escalation, and rollback; ensure dashboards show golden signals (latency, errors, saturation) for affected services.

interview.q: Beginner mistake with Graph Databases?
interview.a: Copying a tutorial config without adapting to your network, secrets, and compliance boundaries.

interview.q: Intermediate: multi-environment strategy?
interview.a: Separate state/credentials per env, promote artifacts not ad-hoc edits, and keep prod changes behind approval gates.

interview.q: Advanced: blast radius control?
interview.a: Batch size limits, feature flags, automatic rollback on SLO burn, and isolated failure domains.

bestPractices: Version-control all Graph Databases configuration and review changes like application code.
Automate validation (lint, policy checks) in CI before production apply.
Document ownership, on-call runbooks, and rollback for every production change.
Use least privilege for credentials and rotate secrets regularly.
Measure before/after: error rate, deploy time, cost, or toil hours saved.
mistakes: Adopting Graph Databases without a written problem statement or success metric.
Skipping staging that mirrors production networking and data volume.
No rollback path — forward-only changes during incidents.
Alert noise without actionable runbooks — on-call ignores pages.
